text: David wondered aloud whether anyone from Saul's household was still living, so that he could show them favor for Jonathan's sake.
A former steward of Saul's house named Ziba was brought before the king, and David asked him this very question.
David asked if any of Saul's family remained to whom he could show the kindness of God; Ziba answered that a son of Jonathan was still alive, disabled in both feet.
Asked where the son could be found, Ziba said he was staying at the home of Machir son of Ammiel in the town of Lo-debar.
So King David sent messengers to bring him from Machir's house in Lo-debar.
When Mephibosheth, Jonathan's son and Saul's grandson, came before David, he bowed low with his face to the ground, and David spoke his name.

David told him not to be afraid, for he intended to show him kindness for his father Jonathan's sake — he would give back all the property that had belonged to Saul, and Mephibosheth would always eat at the king's own table.
Mephibosheth bowed again and asked why the king would notice a lowly man like him, no better than a dead dog.
David then called for Ziba, Saul's former servant, and told him that everything belonging to Saul and his family was now given to his grandson.

Ziba, his sons, and his household servants were to work that land and bring in its harvest so that Mephibosheth's family would have food; meanwhile Mephibosheth himself would always eat at the king's table. Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.
Ziba agreed to do all the king had commanded, and Mephibosheth ate at David's table like one of the king's own sons.
Mephibosheth had a young son named Mica, and everyone in Ziba's household became Mephibosheth's servants.
So Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, since he always ate at the king's table, even though both of his feet were disabled.
